_comment: GENERATED from data/model.json by data/generate.py - do not edit by hand. Nodes are get-off points (a fixed set of moral assumptions); edges are crucial-consideration flips. Every node ranks the SAME donation slate; only the assumptions change, so 'top_pick' changes as you ride down. 'figures' names the public people who most prominently articulate that stop's worldview. The tree forks at s3_inverts into a soil-animal branch (A) and a longtermist branch (F).

nodes.desc: Least-crazy end of the line. Moral weight only on people near me; heavy pure-time discount; humans only; only near-certain, measurable effects count. Under these assumptions distant strangers, animals and the future are discounted to ~0, so the local/civic option wins. The pre-EA commonsense baseline; no single EA figure defends it.

Flip: drop the nationality discount (Peter Singer's famine argument is the origin). A life saved abroad counts like a life saved at home, so cheap, measurable global-health and cash interventions dominate. Alexander Berger is the prominent exemplar of deliberately getting off the train here and staying with robust, evidence-backed global health & wellbeing (GiveWell / Open Phil).

Flip: extend the moral circle to farmed vertebrates, weighted by a sentience-adjusted welfare range. Peter Singer (Animal Liberation) is the canonical articulator; Lewis Bollard leads Open Phil's farm-animal-welfare work. Corporate welfare campaigns avert enormous suffering per dollar and overtake human global health under most welfare-range estimates.

Flip: count invertebrates, with welfare range ~ (neuron count)^exponent. Brian Tomasik is the prominent voice on insect / invertebrate suffering. This is the worked example of the train's instability: sweeping the exponent from 0 to 2 swings shrimp welfare range across ~12 orders of magnitude and flips the ranking (edit neuron_exponent in the node's Squiggle model to watch the top pick move). It is the FORK point: the tree splits here into a soil-animal branch (A) and a longtermist branch (F).

Branch A (biological micro-minds): once you count the ~10^19 soil animals affected by land use, GiveWell top charities' cropland effect works out to ~1.11 kQALY/$ (~1.74x the Shrimp Welfare Project Humane Slaughter Initiative). The 'normal' human charity leaps to ~1.11e5x GiveWell's own direct value - and its SIGN is uncertain (it could be net-negative for soil nematodes). This branch has no Squiggle model yet: there is no comparable published soil-animal BOTEC to copy, so it is not fabricated.

Branch F (longtermist): stop discounting future lives. Toby Ord (The Precipice) and Will MacAskill (What We Owe The Future) are the prominent longtermists; Ajeya Cotra coined the 'train to crazy town' metaphor and rides roughly to here via worldview diversification, taking the future seriously while flagging where it gets uncomfortable. Reducing global-catastrophic risk (e.g. ALLFED's civilisational-resilience work) starts to dominate present-focused giving. The fanatical x-risk tail is still refused here (accept_tiny_prob: false).

Last marked stop on branch F: accept Nick Bostrom's astronomical-waste argument and take small-probability / huge-payoff bets at face value; Eliezer Yudkowsky pushes AI-risk reasoning to its stark conclusions. Existential-risk reduction dominates everything. This is the far end Cotra's near-termist deliberately gets off before; ride further and you reach fanaticism / Pascal's-mugging territory.
